content.problemStatement:
For an array BBB of length NNN, we define f(B)f(B)f(B) as follows.

Consider the following process:


Initially, you have a **bag** containing the elements B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \dots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​.

A bag treated as a multiset, meaning it may contain duplicate values. Your score is initially 000.
N−1N-1N−1 times, perform the following operation:

Choose and remove two elements XXX and YYY from the bag.
Add max⁡(X,Y)\max(X,Y)max(X,Y) to your score.
Insert Z=X & YZ = X\ \&\ YZ=X & Y back into the bag.
In particular, the size of the bag will decrease by exactly 111 after each operation.


f(B)f(B)f(B) is defined to be the **maximum** possible final score you can attain as a result of this process.

You are given two integers NNN and MMM.

Your task is to compute the sum of f(B)f(B)f(B) across all integer arrays of length NNN whose elements lie in [1,M][1, M][1,M].

That is, compute

∑B∈[1,M]Nf(B)\sum_{B \in [1,M]^N} f(B)B∈[1,M]N∑​f(B)Since the answer can be large, output it modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.


Input Format

The first line contains an integer TTT denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two space-separated integers NNN and MMM.


Output Format

For each test case, print a single integer — the value of ∑B∈[1,M]Nf(B)(mod109+7)\sum_{B \in [1,M]^N} f(B) \pmod{10^9+7}∑B∈[1,M]N​f(B)(mod109+7).
constraints: The sum of MMM over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We showcase one optimal sequence of operations for the initial array B=[4,5,6]B = [4, 5, 6]B=[4,5,6].
Initial bag: {4,5,6}\{4,5,6\}{4,5,6}, score =0=0=0
We perform N−1=2N-1 = 2N−1=2 operations.
Operation 1: choose X=4X=4X=4, Y=6Y=6Y=6

Remove 444 and 666 ⇒\Rightarrow⇒ bag becomes {5}\{5\}{5}
Add max⁡(4,6)=6\max(4,6)=6max(4,6)=6 ⇒\Rightarrow⇒ score =6=6=6
Insert (4&6)=4(4 \& 6)=4(4&6)=4 ⇒\Rightarrow⇒ bag becomes {5,4}\{5,4\}{5,4}

Operation 2: choose X=4X=4X=4, Y=5Y=5Y=5

Remove 444 and 555 ⇒\Rightarrow⇒ bag becomes {}\{\}{}
Add max⁡(4,5)=5\max(4,5)=5max(4,5)=5 ⇒\Rightarrow⇒ score =11=11=11
Insert (4&5)=4(4 \& 5)=4(4&5)=4 ⇒\Rightarrow⇒ bag becomes {4}\{4\}{4}

Final score =11=11=11.
There are MN=63=216M^N = 6^3 = 216MN=63=216 possible initial arrays.
It can be verified that the sum of scores across them all equals 182718271827.
Test case 222: We showcase one optimal sequence of operations for the initial array B=[1,2]B = [1, 2]B=[1,2].
Initial bag: {1,2}\{1,2\}{1,2}, score =0=0=0
We can only perform N−1=1N-1 = 1N−1=1 operation, which adds max⁡(1,2)=2\max(1, 2) = 2max(1,2)=2 to the score and inserts 1 & 2=01\ \&\ 2 = 01 & 2=0 back into the bag.
So, the final score is forced to be 222.
It can be seen that the arrays [1,2],[2,1],[2,2][1, 2], [2, 1], [2, 2][1,2],[2,1],[2,2] all have a final score of 222, while [1,1][1, 1][1,1] has a score of 111.
Summing all these up gives 2+2+2+1=72+2+2+1 = 72+2+2+1=7.
